entries.description: The width of lines logged when `config.log` is used.

entries.name: config.missing_image_callback
entries.description:
If not None, this function is called when an attempt to load an
image fails. The callback is passed the filename of the missing image.
It may return None, or it may return an `image manipulator <im>`.
If an image manipulator is returned, that image
manipulator is loaded in the place of the missing image.

One may want to also define a `config.loadable_callback`,
especially if this is used with a `DynamicImage`.

entries.name: config.missing_label_callback
entries.description:
If not None, this function is called when Ren'Py attempts to access
a label that does not exist in the game. The callback should take a
single parameter, the name of the missing label. It should return the
name of a label to use as a replacement for the missing label, or None
to cause Ren'Py to raise an exception.

entries.name: config.profile
entries.description:
If set to True, some profiling information will be output to
stdout.

entries.name: config.profile_init
entries.description:
`init` and `init python` blocks taking longer than this amount of time
to run are reported to log file.

entries.name: config.raise_image_exceptions
entries.description:
If True, Ren'Py will raise an exception when an image name is
not found. If False, Ren'Py will display a textual error message in place of the image.

If None, this takes on the value of config.developer.

This is set to False when Ren'Py ignores an exception.

entries.name: config.raise_image_load_exceptions
entries.description:
If True, Ren'Py will raise an exception when an exception happens
during image loading. If False, Ren'Py will display a textual error
message in place of the image.

If None, this takes on the value of config.developer.

This is set to False when Ren'Py ignore an exception.

entries.name: config.return_not_found_label
entries.description:
If not None, a label that is jumped to when a return site is not found.
The call stack is cleared before this jump occurs.

entries.name: config.gc_print_unreachable
entries.description:
If True, Ren'Py will print to its console and logs information about the
objects that are triggering collections.

entries.name: config.gc_thresholds
entries.description:
The GC thresholds that Ren'Py uses when not idle. These are set to try
to ensure that garbage collection doesn't happen. The three numbers are:

* The net number of objects that need to be allocated before a level-0
  collection.
* The number of level-0 collections that trigger a level-1 collection.
* The number of level-1 collections that trigger a level-2 collection.

(Level-0 collections should be fast enough to not cause a frame drop,
level-1 collections might, level-2 will.)

entries.name: config.idle_gc_count
entries.description:
The net number of objects that triggers a collection when Ren'Py has
reached a steady state. (The fourth frame or later after the screen has been
updated.)

entries.name: config.manage_gc
entries.description:
If True, Ren'Py will manage the GC itself. This means that it will apply
the settings below.

entries.name: config.autoreload
entries.description:
If True, Shift+R will toggle automatic reloading. When automatic
reloading is enabled, Ren'Py will reload the game whenever a used
file is modified.

If False, Ren'Py will reload the game once per press of Shift+R.

entries.name: config.reload_modules
entries.description:
A list of strings giving the names of python modules that should be
reloaded along with the game. Any submodules of these modules
will also be reloaded.

entries.name: gui.text_color
entries.description: This sets the color of the dialogue text.

entries.name: gui.text_font
entries.description:
This sets the font that is used for dialogue text, menus, inputs, and
other in-game text. The font file should exist in the game directory.

entries.name: gui.text_size
entries.description:
Sets the size of the dialogue text. This may need to be increased or
decreased to fit the selected font in the space allotted.

entries.name: gui.name_text_size
entries.description: Sets the size of character names.

entries.name: gui.choice_button_text_idle_color
entries.description: The color used for the text of unfocused choice buttons.

entries.name: gui.choice_button_text_hover_color
entries.description: The color used for the text of focused choice buttons.

entries.name: gui.accent_color
entries.description:
The accent color is used in many places in the GUI, including titles
and labels.

entries.name: gui.idle_color
entries.description: The color used for most buttons when not focused or selected.

entries.name: gui.idle_small_color
entries.description:
The color used for small text (like the date and name of a save slot,
and quick menu buttons) when not hovered. This color often needs to be a
bit lighter or darker than idle_color to compensate for the smaller size
of the font.

entries.name: gui.hover_color
entries.description:
The color used by focused items in the GUI, including the text of
of buttons and the thumbs (movable areas) of sliders and scrollbars.

entries.name: gui.selected_color
entries.description:
The color used by the text of selected buttons. (This takes priority
over the hover and idle colors.)

entries.name: gui.insensitive_color
entries.description:
The color used by the text of buttons that are insensitive to user input.
(For example, the rollback button when no rollback is possible.)

entries.name: gui.interface_text_color
entries.description:
The color used by static text in the game interface, such as text on the
help and about screens.

entries.name: gui.hover_muted_color
entries.description:
Muted colors, used for the sections of bars, scrollbars, and sliders that
do not represent the value or visible area. (These are only used when
generating images, and will not take effect until images are regenerated
in the launcher.)

entries.name: gui.interface_text_font
entries.description:
The font used for text for user interface elements, like the main and
game menus, buttons, and so on.

entries.name: gui.system_font
entries.description:
The font used for system text, like exception messages and the shift+A
accessibility menu. This should be able to handle both ASCII and the
game's translated language.

entries.name: gui.glyph_font
entries.description:
A font used for certain glyphs, such as the arrow glyphs used by the skip
indicator. DejaVuSans is a reasonable default for these glyphs, and is
automatically included with every Ren'Py game.

entries.name: gui.interface_text_size
entries.description:
The size of static text in the game's user interface, and the default size
of button text in the game's interface.

entries.name: gui.label_text_size
entries.description: The size of section labels in the game's user interface.

entries.name: gui.notify_text_size
entries.description: The size of notification text.

entries.name: gui.title_text_size
entries.description: The size of the game's title.

entries.name: gui.frame_borders
entries.description: The borders applied to frame windows.

entries.name: gui.confirm_frame_borders
entries.description: The borders applied to the fame used in the confirm screen.

entries.name: gui.frame_tile
entries.description:
If true, the sides and center of the confirm screen are tiled. If false,
they are scaled.

entries.name: gui.button_height
entries.description:
The width and height of a button, in pixels. If None, the size is
automatically determined based on the size of the text inside a button,
and the borders given below.

entries.name: gui.button_borders
entries.description: The borders surrounding a button, in left, top, right, bottom order.

entries.name: gui.button_tile
entries.description:
If true, the sides and center of the button background are tiled to
increase or  decrease their size. If false, the sides and center are
scaled.

entries.name: gui.button_text_size
entries.description: The font and size of the button text.

entries.name: gui.button_text_insensitive_color
entries.description: The color of the button text in various states.

entries.name: gui.button_text_xalign
entries.description:
The horizontal alignment of the button text. 0.0 is left-aligned,
0.5 is centered, and 1.0 is right-aligned.

entries.name: gui.button_image_extension
entries.description:
The extension for button images. This could be changed to .webp
to use WEBP button images instead of png ones.

entries.name: gui.navigation_button_width
entries.description: Increases the width of navigation buttons.

entries.name: gui.check_button_borders
entries.description:
Increases the width of radio and check button borders, leaving extra
space on the left for the check mark.

entries.name: gui.slot_button_height
entries.description: The width and height of the save slot button.

entries.name: gui.slot_button_borders
entries.description: The borders applied to each save slot.

entries.name: gui.file_slot_rows
entries.description: The number of columns and rows in the grid of save slots.

entries.name: gui.slider_size
entries.description: The height of horizontal sliders, and width of vertical sliders.

entries.name: gui.slider_tile
entries.description:
If true, the frame containing the bar of a slider is tiled. If False,
if it scaled.

entries.name: gui.vslider_borders
entries.description: The borders that are used with the Frame containing the bar image.

entries.name: gui.scrollbar_size
entries.description: The height of horizontal scrollbars, and width of vertical scrollbars.

entries.name: gui.scrollbar_tile
entries.description:
If true, the frame containing the bar of a scrollbar is tiled. If False,
if it scaled.

entries.name: gui.vscrollbar_borders
entries.description: The borders that are used with the Frame containing the bar image.

entries.name: gui.unscrollable
entries.description:
This controls what to do if the bar is unscrollable. "hide" hides
the bar, while None keeps it shown.

entries.name: gui.bar_size
entries.description: The height of horizontal bars, and width of vertical bars.

entries.name: gui.bar_tile
entries.description:
If true, the bar images are tiled. If false, the images are linearly
scaled.

entries.name: gui.vbar_borders
entries.description: The borders that are used with the Frames containing the bar images.

entries.name: gui.skip_frame_borders
entries.description: The borders of the frame that is used by the skip screen.

entries.name: gui.notify_frame_borders
entries.description: The borders of the frame that is used by the notify screen.

entries.name: gui.skip_ypos
entries.description:
The vertical position of the skip indicator, in pixels from the top of the
window.

entries.name: gui.notify_ypos
entries.description:
The vertical position of the notify message, in pixels from the top of the
window.

entries.name: gui.textbox_height
entries.description:
The height of the textbox window, which should also be the height of gui/
textbox.png.

entries.name: gui.textbox_yalign
entries.description:
The placement of the textbox vertically on the screen. 0.0 is the top,
0.5 is center, and 1.0 is the bottom.

entries.name: gui.name_ypos
entries.description:
The horizontal and vertical positions of the name and namebox. These
are usually a number of pixels from the left or top side of the textbox.
Setting a variable to 0.5 centers the name in the textbox (see below).
These numbers can also be negative – for example, setting gui.name_ypos
to -22 causes it to be places 22 pixels above the top of the textbox.

entries.name: gui.name_xalign
entries.description:
The horizontal alignment of the character's name. This can be 0.0 for left-
aligned, 0.5 for centered, and 1.0 for right-aligned. (It's almost always
0.0 or 0.5.) This is used for both the position of the namebox relative
to gui.name_xpos, and to select the side of of the namebox that is aligned
with xpos.

entries.name: gui.namebox_tile
entries.description: These variables control the display of the frame containing the namebox.

entries.name: gui.dialogue_ypos
entries.description:
The horizontal and vertical positions of the actual dialogue. These
are usually a number of pixels from the left or top side of the textbox.
Setting a variable to 0.5 centers the dialogue in the textbox (see below).

entries.name: gui.dialogue_width
entries.description:
This variable gives the maximum width of a line of dialogue, in pixels.
When dialogue reaches this width, it will be wrapped by Ren'Py.

entries.name: gui.dialogue_text_xalign
entries.description:
The horizontal alignment of dialogue text. 0.0 is left aligned, 0.5 is
centered, and 1.0 is right-aligned.

entries.name: gui.history_height
entries.description:
The height of a history entry, in pixels. This can be None to allow
the height of a history entry to vary at the cost of performance –
config.history_length may need to be lowered significantly when this
is None.

entries.name: gui.history_spacing
entries.description: The amount of space to leave between history entries, in pixels.

entries.name: gui.history_text_xpos
entries.description:
The horizontal positions of the name label and dialogue text. These
can be a number of pixels from the left side of the history entry,
or 0.5 to center.

entries.name: gui.history_text_ypos
entries.description:
The vertical positions of the name label and dialogue text, relative
to the top of a history entry, in pixels.

entries.name: gui.history_text_width
entries.description: The width of the name label and dialogue text, in pixels.

entries.name: gui.history_text_xalign
entries.description:
This controls the alignment of text and the side of the text that is
aligned with xpos. 0.0 is left-aligned, 0.5 is center-aligned, 1.0 is
right-aligned.

entries.name: gui.nvl_borders
entries.description:
The borders around the background of the NVL-mode. Since the
background is not a frame, this is only used to pad out the NVL-mode
to prevent it from pressing up against the sides of the screen.

entries.name: gui.nvl_height
entries.description:
The height of a single NVL-mode entry. Setting this to a fixed height
makes it possible to have NVL-mode without paging, showing a fixed number
of entries at once. Setting this to None allows entries to be of a
variable size.

entries.name: gui.nvl_spacing
entries.description:
The spacing between entries when gui.nvl_height is None, and the spacing
between NVL-mode menu buttons.

entries.name: gui.nvl_thought_xpos
entries.description:
The positioning of character names, dialogue text, and thought/narration
text, relative to the left side of the entry. This can be a number of
pixels, or 0.5 to represent the center of the entry.

entries.name: gui.nvl_thought_xalign
entries.description:
The alignment of the text. This controls both the alignment of the text,
and the side of the text that is placed at xpos. This can be 0.0 for left,
0.5 for center, and 1.0 for right.

entries.name: gui.nvl_thought_ypos
entries.description:
The position of character names, dialogue text, and thought/narration text,
relative to the top of the entry. This should be a number of pixels from
the top.

entries.name: gui.nvl_thought_width
entries.description: The width of each kind of text, in pixels.

entries.name: gui.nvl_button_xalign
entries.description: The position and alignment of NVL-mode menu buttons.

entries.name: gui.kind_text_font
entries.description: If present, the font used for the text.

entries.name: gui.kind_text_size
entries.description: If present, the size of the text.

entries.name: gui.kind_text_color
entries.description: If present, the color of the text.

entries.name: gui.show_name
entries.description:
This should be set to False to hide the title and version number from
the main menu. (Say, because the title is "baked into" the main menu
image.)

entries.name: gui.about
entries.description:
Additional text that is added to the about screen. If you want multiple
paragraphs of credits, \\n\\n can be used to separate the paragraphs.

entries.name: build.name
entries.description:
This is used to automatically generate build.directory_name
and build.executable_name, if neither is set. This should not
contain spaces, colons, or semicolons.

entries.name: build.directory_name
entries.description:
This is used to create the names of directories in the archive
files. For example, if this is set to "mygame-1.0", the Linux
version of the project will unpack to "mygame-1.0-linux".

This is also used to determine the name of the directory in
which the package files are placed. For example, if you set
build.directory_name to mygame-1.0, the archive files will
be placed in mygame-1.0-dists in the directory above the base
directory.

This variable should not contain special characters like spaces,
colons, and semicolons. If not set, it defaults to `build.name`,
a dash, and the version. The version is taken from `build.version`,
if set, or `config.version`.

entries.name: build.executable_name
entries.description:
This variable controls the name of the executables that the user
clicks on to start the game.

This variable should not contain special characters like spaces,
colons, and semicolons. If not set, it defaults to `build.name`.

For example, if this is set to "mygame", the user will be able
to run mygame.exe on Windows, mygame.app on Macintosh, and
mygame.sh on Linux.

entries.name: build.time
entries.description:
This variable defaults to None, but if your game has been built,
it will be set to the time the game was built, in seconds since
January 1, 1970.

entries.name: build.info
entries.description:
This variable lets you store information that will be placed into
the game/cache/build_info.json file in the built game. When the built
game starts, game/cache/build_info.json is loaded and the contents
placed into this variable.

Generally, you'll want to check that a field does not exist, and
set it, using setdefault.

For example, this stores the name of the computer that built the
game in the build_info.json file::

    python hide:
        import socket
        build.info.setdefault("build_host", socket.gethostname())

The information in this variable needs to be of types that can be
placed in JSON files. (That is, None, booleans, strings,
numbers, lists, and dictionaries)

entries.name: build.allow_integrated_gpu
entries.description:
Allows Ren'Py to run on the integrated GPU on platforms that have both
integrated and discrete GPUs. Right now, this is only supported on Mac
OS X.

entries.name: build.destination
entries.description:
Gives the path to the directory the archive files will be placed in. This
may be an absolute or a relative path. A relative path is considered to
be relative to the projects directory.

The following values are substituted in using Python's `str.format` function.

`{directory_name}`
    The value of build.directory_name.

`{executable_name}`
    The value of build.executable_name.

`{version}`
    The value of build.version.

entries.name: build.change_icon_i686
entries.description:
If True, and icon.ico exists, the icon of the 32-bit Windows executable
will be changed. If False, the icon will not be changed. Setting this
to False may prevent some antivirus programs from producing a false
positive for your game.

entries.name: build.exclude_empty_directories
entries.description:
If true, empty directories (including directories left empty by
file archiving) will be removed from generated packages. If false,
empty directories will be included.

entries.name: build.game_only_update
entries.description:
If true, `build.include_update` is enabled, and
the "Game-Only Update for Mobile" package becomes available.

entries.name: build.include_i686
entries.description:
If true, files necessary to run on 32-bit x86 processors will be included
in the Linux and Mac builds. If False, these files will not be included.

entries.name: build.include_old_themes
entries.description:
When true, files required to support themes that existed before Ren'Py
6.99.9 will be included in the build. When false, such files are excluded.

This is set to False when `gui.init` is called.

entries.name: build.include_update
entries.description:
When true, Ren'Py will produce the files required for the `updater <updater>`
to work.

entries.name: build.itch_project
entries.description:
Setting this allows the Ren'Py launcher to upload your project to
itch.io. This should be set to the name of a project registered
with itch. (For example, "renpytom/the-question").

Once this is set, after the distributions have been built, you can
click "Build distributions", "Upload to itch.io" to cause an upload
to occur.

entries.name: build.itch_channels
entries.description:
This maps a filename pattern (such as "\*-win.zip") to a string giving
the itch channel the file should be uploaded to. This defaults to::

    {
        "*-all.zip" : "win-osx-linux",
        "*-market.zip" : "win-osx-linux",
        "*-pc.zip" : "win-linux",
        "*-win.zip" : "win",
        "*-mac.zip" : "osx",
        "*-linux.tar.bz2" : "linux",
        "*-release.apk" : "android",
    }

entries.name: build.mac_info_plist
entries.description:
This is a dictionary mapping strings to strings, that can be used to
add or override keys in the mac's Info.plist file.

entries.name: build.update_formats
entries.description:
This is a list of formats that the updater will build. The default,
"rpu" is supported from Ren'Py 7.7 and 8.2 on. If you need to support
updating using the earlier zsync-based updates, add "zsync' to the
list.

entries.name: build.version
entries.description:
Gives a version of the build used by the build process. If None, this defaults to config.version. The main use
of this is to allow config.version to have characters that are not valid in file or directory names.

entries.name: center
entries.description: Centers horizontally, and aligns to the bottom of the screen.

entries.name: default
entries.description:
Centers horizontally, and aligns to the bottom of the screen. This can be
redefined via `config.default_transform` to change the default
placement of images shown with the show or scene statements.

entries.name: left
entries.description: Aligns to the bottom-left corner of the screen.

entries.name: offscreenleft
entries.description:
Places the displayable off the left side of the screen, aligned to the
bottom of the screen.

entries.name: offscreenright
entries.description:
Places the displayable off the right side of the screen, aligned to the
bottom of the screen.

entries.name: reset
entries.description:
Resets the transform to the default values of each property, removing any
properites set before it.

entries.name: right
entries.description: Aligns to the bottom-right corner of the screen.

entries.name: top
entries.description: Centers horizontally, and aligns to the top of the screen.

entries.name: topleft
entries.description: Aligns to the top-left corner of the screen.

entries.name: topright
entries.description: Aligns to the top-right corner of the screen.

entries.name: truecenter
entries.description: Centers both horizontally and vertically.

entries.name: Transform
entries.description:
Creates a transform which applies operations such as cropping, rotation,
scaling or alpha-blending to its child. A transform object has fields
corresponding to the `transform properties <transform_properties>`,
which it applies to its child.

`child`
    The child the transform applies to.

`reset`
    If True, the transform will reset properties to their default values
    when it is shown, rather than inheriting those properties from the
    transforms it replaces.

.. function:: function(trans: Transform, st: float, at: float, /) -> float|None

    If not None, this function will be called when the transform is
    rendered, with three positional arguments:

    * The transform object.
    * The shown timebase, in seconds.
    * The animation timebase, in seconds.

    The function should return a delay, in seconds, after which it will be
    called again, or None to be called again at the start of the next
    interaction.

    This function should not have side effects other than changing the
    Transform object in the first argument, and may be called at any time
    with any value as a part of prediction.

Additional keyword arguments are values that transform properties are set
to. These particular transform properties will be set each time the
transform is drawn, and so may not be changed after the Transform object is
created. Fields corresponding to other transform properties, however, can be
set and changed afterwards, either within the function passed as the
`function` parameter, or immediately before calling the `update`
method.

.. attribute:: hide_request

    This attribute is set to true when the function is called, to indicate
    that the transform is being hidden.

.. attribute:: hide_response

    If `hide_request` is true, this can be set to false to prevent the
    transform from being hidden.

.. attribute:: original_child

    This is the child Displayable that was set when the transform was created,
    before any calls to set_child. This may be None if no child was during creation.

.. method:: set_child(child)

    Call this method with a new `child` to change the child of this
    transform.

.. method:: update()

    This should be called when a transform property field is updated outside
    of the function passed as the `function` argument, to ensure that the
    change takes effect.

.. method:: unique():

    This should be called on a newly created transform to mark it as unique. Usually, transforms are
    copied when when added to a displayable, resetting transform state. Calling this prevents thart
    behavior, and is useful when you want to create a transform that maintains state across multiple
    uses, or that needs to be accessed from outside its `function` argument.

    This function returns the Transform.

entries.name: dissolve
entries.description:
Takes 0.5 seconds to dissolve from the old to the new screen. An instance of
the `Dissolve` transition class.

entries.name: fade
entries.description:
Takes 0.5 seconds to fade to black, and then 0.5 seconds to fade to the new
screen. An instance of the `Fade` transition class.

entries.name: pixellate
entries.description:
Pixellates the old scene for .5 seconds, and the new scene for another .5
seconds. An instance of the `Pixellate` transition class.

entries.name: move
entries.description:
Takes 0.5 seconds to the move images that have changed location to their new
locations. An instance of the `MoveTransition` transition class.

Move transitions, and similar transitions like ease, can only be applied to
a single layer or all layers at once, using the
`with statement <with-statement>`. It will not work in other contexts
such as `ATL <displayable-atl-statement>`, `ComposeTransition`,
or other ways of applying transitions.

entries.name: moveinright
entries.description:
Also: **moveinleft, moveintop, moveinbottom**

These move entering images onto the screen from the appropriate side, taking
0.5 seconds to do so.

entries.name: moveoutright
entries.description:
Also: **moveoutleft, moveouttop, moveoutbottom**

These move leaving images off the screen via the appropriate side, taking
0.5 seconds to do so.

entries.name: ease
entries.description:
Also: **easeinright, easeinleft, easeintop, easeinbottom, easeoutright, easeoutleft, easeouttop, easeoutbottom**

These are similar to the move- family of transitions, except that they use a
cosine-based curve to slow down the start and end of the transition.

entries.name: zoomin
entries.description: This zooms in entering images, taking 0.5 seconds to do so.

entries.name: zoomout
entries.description: This zooms out leaving images, taking 0.5 seconds to do so.

entries.name: zoominout
entries.description:
This zooms in entering images and zooms out leaving images, taking 0.5
seconds to do so.

entries.name: vpunch
entries.description:
When invoked, this transition shakes the screen vertically for a quarter
second. Imitating and customizing this transition and `hpunch` is best
done using `atl-transitions`.

entries.name: hpunch
entries.description:
When invoked, this transition shakes the screen horizontally for a quarter
second.

entries.name: blinds
entries.description:
Transitions the screen in a vertical blinds effect lasting 1 second. An
instance of the `ImageDissolve` transition class.

entries.name: squares
entries.description: Transitions the screen in a squares effect lasting 1 second.

entries.name: wipeleft
entries.description:
Also: **wiperight, wipeup, wipedown**

Wipes the scene in the given direction. Instances of the `CropMove`
transition class.

entries.name: slideleft
entries.description:
Also: **slideright, slideup, slidedown**

Slides the new scene in the given direction. Instances of the
`CropMove` transition class.

entries.name: slideawayleft
entries.description:
Also: **slideawayright, slideawayup, slideawaydown**

Slides the old scene in the given direction. Instances of the
`CropMove` transition class.

entries.name: pushright
entries.description:
Also: **pushleft, pushup, pushdown**

These use the new scene to slide the old scene out the named side. Instances
of the `PushMove` transition class.

entries.name: irisin
entries.description:
Also: **irisout**

Use a rectangular iris to display the new screen, or hide the old screen.
Instances of the `CropMove` transition class.

entries.name: alt
entries.description:
Alternative text used for the displayable when self-voicing is
enabled. See the `self voicing <self_voicing>` section for
more information.

entries.name: xpos
entries.description:
The position of the displayable relative to the left side of the
containing area.

entries.name: ypos
entries.description:
The position of the displayable relative to the top side of the
containing area.

entries.name: pos
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting xpos to the first component of the tuple,
and ypos to the second component of the tuple.

entries.name: xanchor
entries.description:
The position of the anchor relative to the left side of the
displayable.

entries.name: yanchor
entries.description:
The position of the anchor relative to the top side of the
displayable.

If the displayable is a `Text`, then there is one special value
of this property. If the property is set to renpy.BASELINE, the anchor
is set to the baseline of the first line of text.

entries.name: anchor
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting xanchor to the first component of the tuple,
and yanchor to the second component of the tuple.

entries.name: xalign
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting xpos and xanchor to the same value. This has
the effect of placing the displayable at a relative location on
the screen, with 0.0 being the left side, 0.5 the center, and 1.0
being the right side.

entries.name: yalign
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting ypos and yanchor to the same value. This has
the effect of placing the displayable at a relative location on
the screen, with 0.0 being the top, 0.5 the center, and 1.0
the bottom.

entries.name: align
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting xalign to the first component of the tuple,
and yalign to the second.

entries.name: xcenter
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting xpos to the value of this property, and
xanchor to 0.5.

entries.name: ycenter
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting ypos to the value of this property, and
yanchor to 0.5.

entries.name: xycenter
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting xcenter to the first component of the tuple,
and ycenter to the second.

entries.name: xoffset
entries.description:
Gives a number of pixels that are added to the horizontal position
computed using xpos and xalign.

entries.name: yoffset
entries.description:
Gives a number of pixels that are added to the vertical position
computed using ypos and yalign.

entries.name: offset
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting xoffset to the first component of the tuple,
and yoffset to the second component of the tuple.

entries.name: xmaximum
entries.description: Specifies the maximum horizontal size of the displayable, in pixels.

entries.name: ymaximum
entries.description: Specifies the maximum vertical size of the displayable in pixels.

entries.name: maximum
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting xmaximum to the first component of the
tuple, and ymaximum to the second.

entries.name: xminimum
entries.description:
Sets the minimum width of the displayable, in pixels. Only works
with displayables that can vary their size.

entries.name: yminimum
entries.description:
Sets the minimum height of the displayables, in pixels. Only works
with displayables that can vary their size.

entries.name: minimum
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting xminimum to the first component of the
tuple, and yminimum to the second.

entries.name: xsize
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting xminimum and xmaximum to the same value. This
has the effect of setting the width of the displayable.  f the size
is a float, it's relative to the width of the containing area.

entries.name: ysize
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting yminimum and ymaximum to the same value. This
has the effect of setting the height of the displayable. If the size
is a float, it's relative to the height of the containing area.

entries.name: xysize
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting xsize to the first component of
the tuple, and ysize to the second component.

entries.name: xfill
entries.description:
If true, the displayable will expand to fill all available
horizontal space. If not true, it will only be large enough to
contain its children.

This only works for displayables that can change size.

entries.name: yfill
entries.description:
If true, the displayable will expand to fill all available
vertical space. If not true, it will only be large enough to
contain its children.

This only works for displayables that can change size.

entries.name: area
entries.description:
The tuple is interpreted as (`xpos`, `ypos`, `width`,
`height`). Attempts to position the displayable such that it's
upper-left corner is at `xpos` and `ypos`, and its size is `width`
and `height`.

It does this by setting the xpos, ypos, xanchor, yanchor,
xmaximum, ymaximum, xminimum, yminimum, xfill, and yfill
properties to appropriate values.

This will not work with all displayables and all layouts.

entries.name: mipmap
entries.description:
This controls if textures created by this displayable are mipmapped.
This applies only to certain displayables, including `Text`,
`Movie`, and dissolves.

If None, the default for this is taken from config variables such
as `config.mipmap_text`, `config.mipmap_movies`, and
`config.mipmap_dissolves`.

entries.name: antialias
entries.description:
If True, the default, TrueType font text will be rendered
anti-aliased.

entries.name: adjust_spacing
entries.description:
If True, Ren'Py will adjust the spacing of drawable-resolution text
to match the spacing of the text rendered at virtual resolution, to
ensure the size of frames and other containers holding text does not
change.

When set to False, text will not change in size, but it is the creator's
responsibility to ensure there is enough space to layout text at any
window size.

When set to the string "horizontal", text will adjust in the horizontal
direction only. When set to the string "vertical", text will adjust in
the vertical direction only.

Defaults to True for most text, but False for text in an `input`.

entries.name: altruby_style
entries.description:
If not None, this should be a style object. The style that's used for
alternate ruby text.

entries.name: axis
entries.description:
This allows the axes of a `variable font <variable-fonts>` to be
set. If not None, this should be a dictionary mapping axis names to
values. For example::

    style default:
        font "VariableFont.ttf"
        axis { "weight" : 500, "width" : 95 }

entries.name: black_color
entries.description:
When rendering an image-based font, black will be mapped to this
color. This has no effect for TrueType fonts.

This may be None in the case of ruby/furigana text, to use the same
color as the parent text.

entries.name: bold
entries.description:
If True, render the font in a bold style. For a TrueType font,
this usually involves synthetically increasing the font weight. It
can also cause the font to be remapped, using
`config.font_replacement_map`.

entries.name: caret
entries.description:
If not None, this should be a displayable. The input widget will
use this as the caret at the end of the text. If None, a 1 pixel
wide blinking line is used as the caret.

entries.name: color
entries.description:
The color the text is rendered in. When using a TrueType font,
the font is rendered in this color. When using an image-based
font, white is mapped to this color.

This may be None in the case of ruby/furigana text, to use the same
color as the parent text.

entries.name: emoji_font
entries.description:
The font that's used to render Emoji characters. This is automatically
used when a sequence of one or more Emoji are encountered, and no
font text tag is in use.

entries.name: first_indent
entries.description:
The amount that the first line of text in a paragraph is indented
by, in pixels.

entries.name: font
entries.description:
A string giving the name of the font used to render text.

For a TrueType font file, this is usually the name of the file
containing the font (like `"DejaVuSans.ttf"`). To select a second
font in a collection, this can be prefixed with a number and
at sign (like `"0@font.ttc"` or `"1@font.ttc"`). For an
image-based font, this should be the name used to register the
font.

entries.name: font_features
entries.description:
If not None, this should be a dictionary mapping OpenType layout features to values.
The keys of the dictionary should be the four-character OpenType feature tags, and
the values should be booleans.
For example::

    style default:
        font "SomeFont.ttf"
        font_features { "liga" : True, "smcp" : True, "calt" : False }

A list of layout features can be found `here <https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/typography/opentype/spec/featuretags>`_.

entries.name: hinting
entries.description:
Controls how the font will be hinted. This should be one of the following
strings:

"auto"
    The default, forces use of the Freetype auto hinter.
"auto-light"
    Forces the use of the freetype auto hinter in light mode, which only
    hints vertically.
"bytecode"
    Uses bytecode hinting information found in the font.
"none"
    Does not hint the font.

This may also be True, in which case the value is looked up in
`config.font_hinting`.

entries.name: hyperlink_functions
entries.description:
This is a tuple of three functions relating to hyperlinks in text.

The first item is the hyperlink style function. When called with a single
argument, the argument of the hyperlink, it must return a style object to
use for the hyperlink, such as `style.hyperlink_text`. Note that a
style object is not a string.

The second item is the hyperlink clicked function. This function is called
when a hyperlink is chosen by the user. If it returns a value other than
None, the interaction returns that value.

The third item is the hyperlink focus function. This function is called
with the argument of the hyperlink when the hyperlink gains focus, and
with None when it loses focus. If it returns a value other than None,
the interaction returns that value.

entries.name: instance
entries.description:
When using a `variable font <variable-fonts>`, this can be
a string giving a named instance of the font to use. For example,
if the font has a "Bold" instance, this can be set to "Bold" to
use that instance.

entries.name: italic
entries.description:
If true, the text will be rendered in italics. For a TrueType font,
this usually involves synthetically increasing the font slant. It
can also cause the font to be remapped, using
`config.font_replacement_map`.

entries.name: justify
entries.description:
If True, additional whitespace is inserted between words so that
the left and right margins of each line are even. This is not
performed on the last line of a paragraph.

entries.name: kerning
entries.description:
A kerning adjustment, the number of pixels of space that's added
between each pair of characters. (This can be negative to remove
space between characters.)

entries.name: language
entries.description:
Controls the language family used to break text into lines, and
for certain other text transformations. Legal values are:

`"unicode"` (default)
    Uses the Unicode linebreaking algorithm, which is suitable for
    most languages.

`"japanese-strict"`
    Formats Japanese text in a "strict" manner. It
    forbids breaks before small kana and prolonged sound marks.

`"japanese-normal"`
    Formats Japanese text in a "normal" manner. It
    allows breaks before small kana, prolonged sound marks, and
    certain hyphens.

`"japanese-loose"`
    Formats Japanese text in a "loose" manner. It allows breaks
    before small kana , prolonged sound marks, iteration marks,
    inseparable characters, centered punctuation marks, and postfixes;
    and allows breaks before prefixes.

`"korean-with-spaces"`
    Used for Korean text delimited by whitespace. This prevents linebreaking
    between adjacent Korean characters.

`"thaic90"`
    Used for Thai text displayed in fonts that support the C90 encoding.
    It maps character sequences to PUA glyph code points to achieve
    correct glyph positioning. Line breaking uses the Unicode algorithm.

    In most cases, Thai text should use `"unicode"` (the default).
    This is only necessary when using Ren'Py versions earlier than 8.4,
    or when using `"freetype"` as the shaper.

`"western"`
    Allows breaking only at whitespace. Suitable for most
    languages.

`"anywhere"`
    Allows breaking at anywhere without ruby.

The three Japanese breaking modes are taken from the `CSS3 text module <http://www.w3.org/TR/css3-text/#line-break>`_.

entries.name: layout
entries.description:
Controls how words are allocated to lines. Legal values are:

`"tex"` (default)
    Uses the Knuth-Plass linebreaking algorithm, which attempts to minimize
    the difference in line lengths of all but the last line.

`"subtitle"`
    Uses the Knuth-Plass linebreaking algorithm, but attempts to even out
    the lengths of all lines.

`"greedy"`
    A word is placed on the first line that has room for it.

`"nobreak"`
    Do not line-break.

entries.name: line_leading
entries.description: The number of pixels of spacing to include above each line.

entries.name: line_overlap_split
entries.description:
When in slow text mode, and two lines overlap, this many pixels of
the overlap are allocated to the top line. Increase this if the
bottoms of characters on the top line are clipped.

entries.name: line_spacing
entries.description: The number of pixels of spacing to include below each line.

entries.name: min_width
entries.description:
Sets the minimum width of each line of that. If a line is shorter
than this, it is padded to this length, with `textalign` used to
specify where such padding is placed.

entries.name: newline_indent
entries.description:
If true, the `first_indent` indentation is used after
each newline in a string. Otherwise, the `rest_indent`
indentation is used.

entries.name: outlines
entries.description:
This is a list of outlines that are drawn behind the text. Each
tuple specifies an outline, and outlines are drawn from back to
front.

The list contains (`size`, `color`, `xoffset`, `yoffset`)
tuples. `size` is the amount the font is expanded by, in
pixels. `color` is the color of the outline. `xoffset` and
`yoffset` are the amount the outline is shifted by, in pixels.

The outline functionality can also be used to give drop-shadows to
fonts, by specifying a size of 0 and non-zero offsets.

By default, `size`, `xoffset` and `yoffset` are scaled with the text.
When given as the absolute type, they are not scaled. For example::

    style default:
        outlines [ (absolute(1), "#000", absolute(0), absolute(0)) ]

will always produce a 1 pixel-wide border.

Outlines only work with TrueType fonts. Outlines only work when applied
to an entire Text displayable. They do not work when applied to a hyperlink,
text tag, or other method that applies to only a portion of the text.

entries.name: outline_scaling
entries.description:
This determines how outline sizes and offsets are scaled when the
window is scaled.

`"linear"`
    The default, best for text with thick outlines. The window scaling factor
    is applied to the outline size, and then rounded to an integer.
    This looks better for thick outlines, but concentric outlines of similar
    thickness may become indistinguishable.

    The resulting width is always at least 1 pixel.

`"step"`
    Best for text that uses thin outlines and text
    that uses multiple outlines. The window scaling factor is
    rounded down to an integer and applied to the outline size
    and offsets. This ensures that multiple outlines all scale together,
    without risk of eclipsing one another, but this yields different visual
    results depending on the size of the game window.

    The window scaling factor is always above 1.

entries.name: prefer_emoji
entries.description:
Some unicode characters have both Emoji and non-Emoji presentations. This
style property chooses if such characters are given the Emoji presentation
or not.

entries.name: reading_order
entries.description:
This controls the how text is presented for reading when RTL support has
been enabled. The main use of this is to influence directionally neutral
lines, such as those made up purely of punctuation.

`None`
    The default, the reading order will be determined based on the text.
    Most neutral lines will be treated as left-to-right.

`"ltr"`
    Forces left-to-right presentation, even when a line is predominantly
    made up of runs of RTL characters.

`"rtl"`
    Forces right-to-left presentation, even when a line is predominantly
    made up of runs of LTR characters.

`"wltr"`
    Prefers left-to-right presentation, but will switch to right-to-left
    presentation if it makes more sense for the current text. Useful for
    impacting punctuation-only lines such as `"..!"`.

`"wrtl"`
    Prefers right-to-left presentation, but will switch to left-to-right
    presentation if it makes more sense for the current text. Useful for
    impacting punctuation-only lines such as `"..!"`.

entries.name: rest_indent
entries.description:
Specifies the number of pixels the second and later lines in a
paragraph are indented by.

entries.name: ruby_line_leading
entries.description:
The number of pixels of spacing to include above each line that
contains `ruby text <ruby-text>`. This is in addition to
`line_leading`.

entries.name: ruby_style
entries.description:
If not None, this should be a style object. The style that's used for
ruby text.

entries.name: shaper
entries.description:
The shaper used on text. This should be one of "harfbuzz" or "freetype".
The harfbuzz shaper is more capable but only works on Ren'Py 8, while
"freetype" works in Ren'Py 7 as well.

The shaper takes a series of characters and turns it into a series of
positioned glyphs. This is used for things like ligatures, Indic/Bhramic
languages, and combining emjoi.

entries.name: size
entries.description:
The size of the font on the screen. While this is nominally in
pixels, font files may have creative interpretations of this
value.

entries.name: slow_abortable
entries.description:
If True, a click that reaches the Text object will cause the slow
text to abort, which means that the rest of the slow text will be
displayed instantly.

entries.name: slow_cps
entries.description:
If a number, shows text at the rate of that many characters per
second. If True, shows text at the speed taken from the "Text
Speed" preference.

entries.name: slow_cps_multiplier
entries.description:
The speed of the text is multiplied by this number. This can be
used to have a character that speaks at a faster-than-normal rate
of speed.

entries.name: strikethrough
entries.description: If True, a line is drawn through the text.

entries.name: textalign
entries.description:
This is used when a line is shorter than the width of the text
displayable. It determines how much of the extra space is placed
on the left side of the text. (And hence, the text alignment.)

0.0 will yield left-aligned text, 0.5 centered text, and 1.0
right-aligned text.

entries.name: underline
entries.description: If True, an underline will be added to the text.

entries.name: vertical
entries.description:
If True, the text will be rendered vertically.

There are multiple caveats:

* If `shaper` is "freetype", vertical layout will likely be incorrect.

* Harfbuzz will convert horizontal forms to vertical forms if those
  forms are present in the font.

* Characters will be laid out top to bottom, right to left, but will
  not be rotated. This means that horizontal characters will be laid
  out one on top of another.

* If vertical text information is not present in the font, it will be
  synthesized, but may be incorrect. Generally, ideographic text works
  better than non-ideographic text.

entries.name: background
entries.description:
A displayable that is used as the background of the window. This
is often a `Frame`, allowing the size of the background to
scale with the size of the window.

If None, no background is drawn, but other properties function as
if the background was present.

entries.name: foreground
entries.description:
If not None, this displayable is drawn above the contents of the
window.

entries.name: left_padding
entries.description:
The amount of space between the background and the left side of
the window content, in pixels.

entries.name: right_padding
entries.description:
The amount of space between the background and the right side of
the window content, in pixels.

entries.name: xpadding
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting left_padding and right_padding to the same
value.

entries.name: top_padding
entries.description:
The amount of space between the background and the top side of
the window content, in pixels.

entries.name: bottom_padding
entries.description:
The amount of space between the background and the bottom side of
the window content, in pixels.

entries.name: ypadding
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting top_padding and bottom_padding to the same
value.

entries.name: padding
entries.description:
When given a two-item tuple, equivalent to setting xpadding and
ypadding to the two items. When given a four-item tuple, equivalent
to setting left_padding, top_padding, right_padding, and bottom_padding
to the four items.

entries.name: size_group
entries.description:
If not None, this should be a string. Ren'Py will render all
windows with the same size_group value at the same size.

entries.name: modal
entries.description:
If True, the window is treated as modal. Events will stop propagating
through layers while the mouse is within the window or button. If False,
the window is not modal.

This may also be a callable function. If it is, the function is called
with (ev, x, y, w, h), where `ev` is either a pygame event, or None to
represent a generic mouse event; `x` and `y` are coordinates relative
to the window, and `w` and `h` are the height and width of the window.
If the callable returns true, the windows is treated as modal. If it
returns false, it is not.

entries.name: child
entries.description:
If not None, this gives a displayable that replaces the child of the
button. For example, this (as insensitive_child) can be used to replace the
contents of an insensitive button with an image that indicates the button
is locked.

entries.name: hover_sound
entries.description: A sound that is played when the button gains focus.

entries.name: activate_sound
entries.description:
A sound that is played when the button is activated (clicked). This is also
played when a bar or drag is activated.

entries.name: mouse
entries.description:
The mouse style that is used when the button is focused. This
should be one of the styles in `config.mouse`.

entries.name: focus_mask
entries.description:
A mask that's used to control what portions of the button can be
focused, and hence clicked on. The type of this property determines
how it is interpreted.

Displayable
    The areas of the displayable that are not transparent cause the button
    to be focused.
True
    The button itself is used as the displayable (so
    non-transparent areas of the button cause the button to be
    focused).
callable
    If a non-displayable callable (like a function, method, or object
    with a `__call__` method) is given, the function is called with two
    arguments, the x and y offset from the top-left corner of the
    displayable.

    If the function returns a callable when called with two arguments,
    that callable is called with four arguments - the x and y offsets, and the width
    and height of the displayable.

    If the function returns true, the displayable is focused.
None
    If none is given, the entire button can be focused.

entries.name: keyboard_focus_insets
entries.description:
If not None, this should be a tuple of four integers, giving the
number of pixels that are used to shrink the left, top, right, and
bottom sides of the focus rectangle, when it's used for keyboard
focus.

This can be useful when buttons overlap. The keyboard focus algorithm
doesn't work with overlapping buttons, and so this can be used to to
shrink the size of the buttons internally, allowing focus to work.

entries.name: key_events
entries.description:
If True, keyboard-generated events are passed to the children of this
button. If False, those events are not propagated. In this default style,
this is set to True while the button is hovered, and False otherwise.

Setting this to True can be used to propagate keyboard events to an input
inside a button, even when the button isn't focused.

entries.name: bar_vertical
entries.description:
If True, the bar has a vertical orientation. If False, it has a
horizontal orientation.

entries.name: bar_invert
entries.description:
If True, the value of the bar is represented on the right/top
side of the bar, rather than the left/bottom side.

entries.name: bar_resizing
entries.description:
If True, we resize the sides of the bar. If False, we render the
sides of the bar at full size, and then crop them.

entries.name: left_gutter
entries.description: The size of the gutter on the left side of the bar, in pixels.

entries.name: right_gutter
entries.description: The size of the gutter on the right side of the bar, in pixels.

entries.name: top_gutter
entries.description: The size of the gutter on the top side of the bar, in pixels.

entries.name: bottom_gutter
entries.description: The size of the gutter on the bottom side of the bar, in pixels.

entries.name: left_bar
entries.description: The displayable used for the left side of the bar.

entries.name: right_bar
entries.description: The displayable used for the right side of the bar.

entries.name: top_bar
entries.description: The displayable used for the top side of the bar.

entries.name: bottom_bar
entries.description: The displayable uses for the bottom side of the bar.

entries.name: base_bar
entries.description:
A single displayable that is used for left_bar/right_bar or
top_bar/bottom_bar, as appropriate. (This can be used
with thumb to make a slider or scrollbar.)

entries.name: thumb
entries.description:
If not None, this is a displayable that is drawn over the break
between the sides of the bar.

entries.name: thumb_align
entries.description:
The alignment of the bar thumb, relative to the bar. If the bar and
thumb are different sizes - for example, the thumb is taller than the
height of a horizontal bar - thumb_align can be set to 0.5 so the centers
of the bar and thumb are aligned.

entries.name: thumb_shadow
entries.description:
If not None, this is a displayable that is drawn over the break
between the sides of the bar.

entries.name: thumb_offset
entries.description:
The amount that by which the thumb overlaps the bars, in
pixels. To have the left and right bars continue unbroken, set
this to half the width of the thumb in pixels. This may also be
a tuple, in which case the first number is used for the left/top
thumb offset, and the second number is used for the right/bottom
thumb offset.

entries.name: unscrollable
entries.description:
Controls what happens if the bar is unscrollable (if the range is
set to 0, as is the case with a viewport containing a displayable
smaller than itself). There are three possible values:

`None`
    Renders the bar normally.

`"insensitive"`
    Renders the bar in the insensitive state. This allows the
    bar to change its style to reflect its lack of usefulness.

`"hide"`
   Prevents the bar from rendering at all. Space will be allocated
   for the bar, but nothing will be drawn in that space.

entries.name: spacing
entries.description: The spacing between members of this box, in pixels.

entries.name: first_spacing
entries.description:
If not None, the spacing between the first and second members of
this box, in pixels. This overrides the spacing property.

entries.name: box_reverse
entries.description:
If True, the placement of the items in the box will be reversed. When
this is True, a hbox will be filled right-to-left, and a vbox will
be filled bottom-to-top. This defaults to False.

entries.name: box_wrap
entries.description:
If True, then boxes will wrap when they reach the end of a line or column.
If False (the default), they will extend past the end of the line.

entries.name: box_wrap_spacing
entries.description:
When box_wrap is True, this is the spacing between wrapped lines
or columns. (So it is the vertical spacing between lines in a wrapped
hbox, and the horizontal spacing between columns in a wrapped vbox.)

entries.name: box_align
entries.description:
This determines the alignment of rows or columns in a box. If 0.0,
the default, rows are left-aligned and columns are top-aligned. If 0.5,
it will center wrapped rows.

When this is not None, addional space will not be offered to the child when
`xfill` or `yfill` is set, ignoring the child's position
in the box's primary direction.

entries.name: order_reverse
entries.description:
If False, the default, the items in the box will be drawn first-to-last,
with the first item in the box being below the second, and so on. If True,
this order will be reversed, and the first item in the box will be above
all other items in the box.

entries.name: box_justify
entries.description:
If not False, the contents of the box will be justified - that is, the items
inside the box will have the spacing between them adjusted so they evenly
span the size of the box. True will cause all lines but the final line to be
justified. "first" will cause only the first line to be justified. "all"
will cause all lines to be justified, including the final line. Justified
lines do not obey box_align, except when there is only one item in a line.

When this is not false, addional space will not be offered to the child when
`xfill` or `yfill` is set, ignoring the child's position
in the box's primary direction.

entries.name: xspacing
entries.description:
The spacing between cells of the grid in the horizontal direction, in pixels.
This takes precedence over the spacing property when it is not None.

entries.name: yspacing
entries.description:
The spacing between cells of the grid in the vertical direction, in pixels.
This takes precedence over the spacing property when it is not None.

entries.name: fit_first
entries.description:
If True, then the size of the fixed layout is shrunk to be equal with
the size of the first item in the layout. If "width", only the width is changed
(the fixed will fill the screen vertically). Similarly, "height" only changes
the height.

The position of the child is ignored for the purpose of fit\_first.

entries.name: xfit
entries.description:
If True, the size of the fixed layout is shrunk horizontally to match the
right side of the rightmost child of the fixed.

entries.name: yfit
entries.description:
If True, the size of the fixed layout is shrunk vertically to match the
bottom side of the bottommost child of the fixed.

entries.name: left_margin
entries.description:
The amount of transparent space to the left of the displayable, in
pixels.

entries.name: right_margin
entries.description:
The amount of transparent space to the right of the displayable, in
pixels.

entries.name: xmargin
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting left_margin and right_margin to the same
value.

entries.name: top_margin
entries.description: The amount of transparent space above the displayable, in pixels.

entries.name: bottom_margin
entries.description: The amount of transparent space below the displayable, in pixels.

entries.name: ymargin
entries.description:
Equivalent to setting top_margin and bottom_margin to the same
value.

entries.name: margin
entries.description:
When given a two-item tuple, equivalent to setting xmargin and
ymargin to the two items. When given a four-item tuple, equivalent
to setting left_margin, top_margin, right_margin, and bottom_margin
to the four items.

entries.name: subpixel
entries.description:
:type: boolean
:default: False

If True, causes the child to be placed using subpixel positioning.

Subpixel positioning effects the colors (including transparency) that are
drawn into pixels, but not which pixels are drawn. When subpixel positioning
is used in combination with movement (the usual case), the image should have
transparent borders in the directions it might be moved in, if those edges
are visible on the screen.

For example, if a character sprite is being moved horizontally, it makes
sense to have transparent borders on the left and right. These might not be
necessary when panning over a background that extends outside the visible
area, as the edges will not be seen.

entries.name: rotate
entries.description:
:type: float or None
:default: None

If None, no rotation occurs. Otherwise, the image will be rotated by this
many degrees clockwise. Rotating the displayable causes it to be resized,
according to the setting of rotate_pad, below. This can cause positioning to
change if xanchor and yanchor are not 0.5.

entries.name: rotate_pad
entries.description:
:type: boolean
:default: True

If True, then a rotated displayable is padded such that the width and height
are equal to the hypotenuse of the original width and height. This ensures
that the transform will not change size as its contents rotate. If False,
the transform will be given the minimal size that contains the transformed
displayable. This is more suited to fixed rotations.

entries.name: zoom
entries.description:
:type: float
:default: 1.0

This causes the displayable to be zoomed by the supplied factor.

entries.name: xzoom
entries.description:
:type: float
:default: 1.0

This causes the displayable to be horizontally zoomed by the supplied
factor. A negative value causes the image to be flipped horizontally.

entries.name: nearest
entries.description:
:type: boolean
:default: None

If True, the displayable and its children are drawn using nearest-neighbor
filtering. If False, the displayable and its children are drawn using
bilinear filtering. If None, this is inherited from the parent, or
`config.nearest_neighbor`, which defaults to False.

entries.name: alpha
entries.description:
:type: float
:default: 1.0

This controls the opacity of the displayable.

The alpha transform is applied to each image comprising the child of the
transform independently. This can lead to unexpected results when the
children overlap, such as seeing a character through clothing. The
`Flatten` displayable can help with these problems.

entries.name: additive
entries.description:
:type: float
:default: 0.0

This controls how much additive blending Ren'Py performs. When 1.0, Ren'Py
draws using the ADD operator. When 0.0, Ren'Py draws using the OVER
operator.

Additive blending is performed on each child of the transform independently.

Fully additive blending doesn't alter the alpha channel of the destination,
and additive images may not be visible if they're not drawn directly onto an
opaque surface. (Complex operations, like viewport, `Flatten`,
`Frame`, and certain transitions may cause problems with additive
blending.)

entries.name: matrixcolor
entries.description:
:type: None or Matrix or MatrixColor
:default: None

If not None, the value of this property is used to recolor everything that
children of this transform draw. Interpolation is only supported when
MatrixColors are used, and the MatrixColors are structurally similar. See
`matrixcolor` for more information.

entries.name: blur
entries.description:
:type: None or float
:default: None

This blurs the child of this transform by `blur` pixels, up to the border
of the displayable. The precise details of the blurring may change between
Ren'Py versions, and the blurring may exhibit artifacts, especially when the
image being blurred is changing.

Blurring causes its child to be flattened, with the child drawn onto a transparent
background before the blurring happens.

entries.name: around
entries.description:
:type: (position, position)
:default: (0.0, 0.0)

This specifies the starting point, relative to the upper-left corner of the
containing area, from where the polar vector (computed from `angle`
and `radius`) will be drawn. The sum of the two gives the resulting
`pos`.

entries.name: angle
entries.description:
:type: float

This gives the angle component of a position specified in polar coordinates.
This is measured in degrees, with 0 being to the top of the screen, and 90
being to the right.

Ren'Py clamps this angle to between 0 and 360 degrees, including 0 but
not 360. If a value is set outside this range, it will be set to the
equivalent angle in this range before being used. (Setting this to -10 is
the equivalent of setting it to 350.)

entries.name: radius
entries.description:
:type: position

The radius component of the position given in polar coordinates.

If a float, this will be scaled to the smaller of the width and height
available to the transform.

entries.name: anchoraround
entries.description:
:type: (position, position)

This specifies the starting point, relative to the upper-left corner of the
displayable, from where the polar vector (computed from `anchorangle`
and `anchorradius`) will be drawn. The sum of the two gives the
resulting `anchor`.

entries.name: anchorangle
entries.description:
:type: (float)

The angle component of the polar coordinates of the anchor. This is
specified in degrees, with 0 being to the top and 90 being to the right.

Ren'Py clamps this angle to between 0 and 360 degrees, including 0 but
not 360. If a value is set outside this range, it will be set to the
equivalent angle in this range before being used. (Setting this to -10 is
the equivalent of setting it to 350.)

entries.name: anchorradius
entries.description:
:type: (position)

The radius component of the polar coordinates of the anchor.

If a float, it is scaled horizontally and vertically to the size and shape
of the displayable: if the height is not equal to the width, a radius that
is not strictly absolute will result in elliptical motion when varying the
anchorangle. For that reason, it is recommended to only pass `int` or
`absolute` values to this property.

entries.name: crop
entries.description:
:type: None or (position, position, position, position)
:default: None

If not None, causes the displayable to be cropped to the given box. The box
is specified as a tuple of (x, y, width, height), with x and y being the
coordinates of the box's top-left corner relative to the top-left corner of
the child. All values can expand outside of the bounds of the original
image, with the area outside being transparent, though width and height must
be positive.

If corners and crop are given, crop takes priority over corners.

entries.name: corner1
entries.description:
:type: None or (position, position)
:default: None

If not None, gives the upper-left corner of the crop box. The values can
expand outside of the bounds of the original image. Crop takes priority over
corners.

entries.name: corner2
entries.description:
:type: None or (position, position)
:default: None

If not None, gives the lower-right corner of the crop box. The values can
expand outside of the bounds of the original image, but they should not be
inferior to `corner1`. Crop takes priority over corners.

entries.name: fit
entries.description:
:type: None or string
:default: None

Causes the displayable to be sized according to the table below. In the
context of the table below, the "dimensions" are:

* If both `xsize` and `ysize` are not None, both sizes are
  used as the dimensions.
* If only one of those properties is not None, it is used as the sole
  dimension.
* Otherwise, if fit is not None the area that the Transform is contained in
  is used as the dimensions.

If fit, xsize, and ysize are all None, this property does not apply.

.. list-table::
   :widths: 15 85
   :header-rows: 1

   * - Value
     - Description
   * - `contain`
     - As large as possible, without exceeding any dimensions. Maintains
       aspect ratio.
   * - `cover`
     - As small as possible, while matching or exceeding all dimensions.
       Maintains aspect ratio.
   * - None or `fill`
     - Stretches/squashes displayable to exactly match dimensions.
   * - `scale-down`
     - As for `contain`, but will never increase the size of the
       displayable.
   * - `scale-up`
     - As for `cover`, but will never decrease the size of the
       displayable.

entries.name: xpan
entries.description:
:type: None or float
:default: None

If not None, this interpreted as an angle that is used to pan horizontally
across a 360 degree panoramic image. The center of the image is used as the
zero angle, while the left and right edges are -180 and 180 degrees,
respectively.

entries.name: ypan
entries.description:
:type: None or float
:default: None

If not None, this interpreted as an angle that is used to pan vertically
across a 360 degree panoramic image. The center of the image is used as the
zero angle, while the top and bottom edges are -180 and 180 degrees,
respectively.

entries.name: xtile
entries.description:
:type: int
:default: 1

The number of times to tile the image horizontally.

entries.name: ytile
entries.description:
:type: int
:default: 1

The number of times to tile the image vertically.

entries.name: delay
entries.description:
:type: float
:default: 0.0

If this transform is being used as a transition, then this is the duration
of the transition. See `atl-transitions`.

entries.name: events
entries.description:
:type: boolean
:default: True

If True, events are passed to the child of this transform. If False, events
are blocked. (This can be used in ATL transitions to prevent events from
reaching the old_widget.)

entries.name: fps
entries.description:
:type: float or None
:default: None

If not None, this alters time inside the transform so that it is discrete.
For example, if a transform has an fps of 10, then times inside the
transform will be rounded down to the nearest multiple of 0.1. This can be
used to simulate a lower frame rate.

entries.name: show_cancels_hide
entries.description:
:type: boolean
:default: True

Normally, when a displayable or screen with the same tag or name as one that
is hiding is shown, the hiding displayable or screen is removed, cancelling
the hide transform. If this property is False in the hide transform, this
cancellation will not occur, and the hide transform will proceed to
completion.

entries.name: alignaround
entries.description:
:type: (float, float)

This sets `anchor`, `around`, and `anchoraround` to the
same value.

entries.name: crop_relative
entries.description:
:type: boolean
:default: True

If False, float components of `crop`, `corner1` and
`corner2` are interpreted as an absolute number of pixels, instead of
a fraction of the width and height of the source image.

If an absolute number of pixel is to be expressed, `absolute`
instances should be provided to these properties instead of using the
crop_relative property. If necessary, values of dubious type can be wrapped
in the `absolute` callable.

entries.name: maxsize
entries.description:
:type: None or (int, int)
:default: None

If not None, causes the displayable to be scaled so that it fits within a
box of this size, while preserving aspect ratio. (Note that this means that
one of the dimensions may be smaller than the size of this box.)

To achieve the same result, give the values to the `xysize` property,
and set the `fit` property to the value "contain".

entries.name: persistent._hasattr
entries.description:
Tests whether the `field_name` persistent field has been set or not.
This allows you to distinguish fields that have been explicitly set
to None from fields that have never been set.

entries.name: persistent._clear
entries.description:
Resets the persistent data, except for fields starting with `__`.

`progress`
    If true, also resets progress data that Ren'Py keeps.

Note that this will re-apply defaults.

entries.name: MultiPersistent
entries.description:
Creates a new `MultiPersistent` object. This should only be called at init time,
and it returns a new `MultiPersistent` with the given name.

`name`
    The name used to to access the multipersistent data. Games using the
    same name will access the same multipersistent data.

`save_on_quit`
    If it is True, this object will be automatically saved when Ren'Py terminates.

.. method:: save()

    Saves the multipersistent data to disk. This must be called after
    the data is modified.

entries.name: preferences.afm_after_click
entries.description:
If True, the auto-forward mode will be continued after a click. If False,
a click will end auto-forward mode. The equivalent of the "auto-forward
after click" preference.

entries.name: preferences.afm_enable
entries.description:
If True, auto-forward move is enabled, otherwise False. The equivalent
of the "auto-forward time" preference.

entries.name: preferences.afm_time
entries.description:
The amount of time to wait for auto-forward mode. Bigger numbers are
slower, though the conversion to wall time is complicated, as the
speed takes into account line length. The equivalent of the
"auto-forward" preference.

entries.name: preferences.desktop_rollback_side
entries.description:
When on a desktop platform, touches or clicks to this side of the window
cause rollback to occur. One of "left", "right", or "disable". This is
the equivalent of the "rollback side" preference when on a desktop
platform.

entries.name: preferences.mobile_rollback_side
entries.description:
When on a mobile platform, touches or clicks to this side of the window
cause rollback to occur. One of "left", "right", or "disable". This is
the equivalent of the "rollback side" preference when on a mobile
platform.

entries.name: preferences.language
entries.description:
The language that the player has selected to use when running the game.
This is None for the default language or a string containing a language
the game is translated to.

This can be used to set the default language, and can be read to determine
the current language. The `Language` action can be used to change
the language.

See `translation` for more information.

entries.name: preferences.emphasize_audio
entries.description:
If True, Ren'Py will emphasize the audio channels found in `config.emphasize_audio_channels`
by reducing the volume of other channels. (For example, reducing the music volume when voice
is playing.) If False, this doesn't happen.

entries.name: preferences.fullscreen
entries.description:
This is True when Ren'Py is in fullscreen mode, and False when it
is running in a window. The equivalent of the "display" preference.

entries.name: preferences.gl_framerate
entries.description:
This is either an integer, or None. If not None, it's a target framerate
that Ren'Py will attempt to achieve. If this is set low (for example, to
30), on a monitor with a high framerate (say, 60 frames per second),
Ren'Py will only draw on every other frame.

If None, Ren'Py will attempt to draw at the monitor's full framerate.

entries.name: preferences.gl_powersave
entries.description:
This determines how often Ren'Py will redraw an unchanging screen. If True,
Ren'Py will only draw the screen 5 times a second. If False, it will always
draw at the full framerate possible.

entries.name: preferences.gl_tearing
entries.description:
This determines if tearing (True) or frameskip (False) is the preferred
behavior when the game can't keep up with its intended framerate.

entries.name: preferences.mouse_move
entries.description:
If True, the mouse will automatically move to a selected button. If False,
it will not. The equivalent of the "automatic move" preference.

entries.name: preferences.show_empty_window
entries.description:
If True, the window show and window auto statements will function. If
False, those statements are disabled. The equivalent of the "show empty window"
preference.

entries.name: preferences.skip_after_choices
entries.description:
If True, skipping will resume after a choice. If False, a choice will
prevent Ren'Py from skipping. The equivalent of the "after choices"
preference.

entries.name: preferences.skip_unseen
entries.description:
When True, Ren'Py will skip all text. When False, Ren'Py will
only skip text that has been read by the player in any session.
The equivalent of the "skip" preference.

entries.name: preferences.text_cps
entries.description:
The speed of text display. 0 is infinite, otherwise this is the number
of characters per second to show. The equivalent of the "text speed"
preference.

entries.name: preferences.transitions
entries.description:
Determines which transitions should be shown. 2 shows all transitions,
0 shows no transitions. (1 is reserved.) The equivalent of the
"transitions" preference.

entries.name: preferences.video_image_fallback
entries.description:
If True, images are displayed instead of videosprites. If False,
video sprites are displayed normally. The equivalent (inverted) of the
"video sprites" preference.

entries.name: preferences.voice_sustain
entries.description:
If True, voice keeps playing until finished, or another voice line
replaces it. If False, the voice line ends when the line of dialogue
advances. The equivalent of the "voice sustain" preference.

entries.name: preferences.wait_voice
entries.description:
If True, auto-forward mode will wait for voice files and self-voicing to
finish before advancing. If False, it will not. The equivalent of the
"wait for voice" preference.

entries.name: preferences.system_cursor
entries.description:
If True, the system cursor is forced to be used, ignoring the value of
`config.mouse` and `config.mouse_displayable`. If False, it
will not. The equivalent of the "system cursor" preference.

entries.name: preferences.audio_when_minimized
entries.description:
If False, audio channels are stopped when the window is minimized,
and resumed when the window is restored. If True, window state will
have no effect on audio.
The equivalent of the "audio when minimized" preference.

entries.name: preferences.audio_when_unfocused
entries.description:
